coredata.dc:title: Self-functionalized ultrastable water suspension of luminescent carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Arvind
Mohapatra, Pranab Kishore
Kalyanasundaram, Dinesh
Kumar, Sunil
coredata.dc:description: Crystalline graphite microparticles are good source for fabricating carbon based nanodots. In spite of their tremendous applications in biomedical research, the stability and non-toxicity of the product material has not been addressed. In this paper, we show that by nanosecond laser irradiation of graphitic microparticles in water, a highly stable water suspension of luminescent carbon quantum dots of high purity is generated. The sizes of the quantum dots are tuned in the range from 5 nm to 15 nm by varying the laser irradiation time. Quantum confinement is strongly evident from the optical absorption and photoluminescence spectra. Interestingly, we observed the quantum dots are self-functionalized during the fragmentation process. FTIR spectrum shows the presence of unsaturated CO bond at the surface of the quantum dots resulting in the development of similar charges. Due to the repulsive Coulombic force, these quantum dots remain stable for a very long period of time avoiding the use of any unwanted surface ligands or surfactants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphite
Quantum dots
Laser ablation
Optical spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Optical schematic of the experimental set up used forns pulsed laser ablation of the graphitic microparticles dispersed in water. (b) Synthesis of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the laser ablation process. The CQDs are negatively charged owing to the presence of CO functional groups on the surface.

(a) Optical images of the stable CQDs water suspensions as synthesized (top)and after four months (bottom) for S40, S60 and S80 samples. (b) FTIR spectra of the S80 sample taken at the time of preparation and after four months to show presence of the functional groups attached on the surface to the CQDs. (c) Zeta potential results from three consecutive measurements on S40 sample.

TEM images of (a) S40, (b) S60, and (c) S80 CQDs samples. The histogram plots in the insets show the particle size distribution in each case.(d) High resolution TEM image of a single CQD showing crystalline interplanar spacing d of ∼3.8 Å. (e) Selected area electron diffraction pattern to show the polycrystalline nature of the CQDs.
XRD spectra of untreated crystalline graphite microparticles in powder and the three carbon quantum dots samples synthesized by nanosecond laser ablation.

(a)UV–Visible absorption spectra, and(b) Photoluminescence spectra at 325 nm laser excitation of CQDs in the S40, S60 and S80 samples. The dashed lines are drawn to mark the spectral shift. The inset in (a) is the absorption spectrum of graphitic microparticles.

(a) Raman spectra of the laser untreated crystalline graphite microparticles and the CQDs in S40, S60 and S80 water suspensions. Raman spectra along with Lorentzian fits for (b) graphitic microparticles, and (c) CQDs in S80 sample. Signature G-mode and D-mode of graphite are marked in (b) and those for CQDs are shown in (c). The intensity ratios between D and G bands are marked in the inset for all the four samples.

head.ce:title: Self-functionalized ultrastable water suspension of luminescent carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, New Delhi, 110016, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, New Delhi, 110016, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Crystalline graphite microparticles are good source for fabricating carbon based nanodots. In spite of their tremendous applications in biomedical research, the stability and non-toxicity of the product material has not been addressed. In this paper, we show that by nanosecond laser irradiation of graphitic microparticles in water, a highly stable water suspension of luminescent carbon quantum dots of high purity is generated. The sizes of the quantum dots are tuned in the range from 5 nm to 15 nm by varying the laser irradiation time. Quantum confinement is strongly evident from the optical absorption and photoluminescence spectra. Interestingly, we observed the quantum dots are self-functionalized during the fragmentation process. FTIR spectrum shows the presence of unsaturated CO bond at the surface of the quantum dots resulting in the development of similar charges. Due to the repulsive Coulombic force, these quantum dots remain stable for a very long period of time avoiding the use of any unwanted surface ligands or surfactants.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of viable fluorescent nanomaterial was first discovered during the purification of carbon nanotubes [1]. The large surface to volume ratio, low toxicity, chemical stability and fluorescence properties make these quantum dots useful in a number of applications including biomedical and water purification technologies [2–6]. In recent years, CQDs have been synthesized using varieties of approaches and techniques. Broadly, they can be divided into top-down and bottom-up approaches. In top-down methods, CQDs are prepared using laser ablation [7–10], arc discharge [11], chemical oxidation in strong acid [12], or electrochemical synthesis [13], starting from a range of carbon allotropes, such as graphite (powder/solid target), carbon nanotubes and nanodiamonds. The bottom-up synthesis includes processes such as solvothermal treatment [14,15], microwave assisted process [16,17], ultrasonication [18] or template-supported process [19]. Among all the above, the laser ablation technique is quite attractive because of its simple, straightforward and green approach to produce CQDs in a single step. One of the major advantages of this technique is to keep the pristinity of the starting material and to avoid the use of hazardous chemicals.

Using the laser ablation technique, various studies have been reported in the literature on synthesis of CQDs with nanosecond (ns) and femtosecond (fs) laser irradiation of various carbon targets [7–10,20,21]. However, in most of these studies, the use of organic solvents hinders the product material from use in in-vivo biomedical applications. Simple and viable synthesis routines are therefore very much essential for generating, fine tuning of the size and properties of highly stable fluorescent CQDs water suspensions. Recently in a few reports, researchers have been successful in synthesizing water dispersed carbon nanoparticles with average size of ∼50 nm or larger by using ns pulsed laser ablation technique [22]. However, the stability has always been a challenge for the water suspensions which is highly desirable, particularly for biological applications.

Here, we report ns pulsed laser ablation assisted synthesis of ultrahigh stable water suspension of CQDs with size as small as 5 nm without using toxic chemical agents, which has not been reported hitherto. The current work relies on an ecofriendly and single step synthesis process of CQDs, starting from crystalline graphite microparticles dispersed in water. It was observed that the size of the CQDs can be varied by altering the laser irradiation time and maintaining other optimized parameters such as laser power, pulse repetition rate, laser wavelength and pulse duration.

Graphitic microparticles of average size greater than 20 μm were mixed in water to yield a concentration of ∼1 mg/mL. For the laser ablation of the sample in water, a ns pulsed fiber laser operating at wavelength of ∼1070 nm, pulse-duration of ∼50 ns and pulse repetition rate variable between 1 and 500 kHz (SPI lasers UK Ltd.) was used. For the initial experiments, various combinations of laser parameters (laser power, repetition rate and laser spot scanning speed) were tried to synthesize the CQDs. The results discussed below in the paper have been obtained for experimental parameters: average power of ∼40 W and pulse repetition rate 50 kHz. The laser fluence (peak intensity) at the focal point was kept at ∼63 J/cm2 (1.2 GW/cm2) and experiments were carried out for different laser irradiation times, i.e., 40,60, and 80 min. The resultant water dispersions of the CQDs have been accordingly named as S40, S60 and S80, respectively. The laser beam was focused inside the graphite water dispersion at ∼5 mm depth from the liquid–air interface and typical spot size was ∼40 μm. A magnetic stirrer was used for continuous mixing of the laser fragmented graphite particles to avoid their precipitation. A schematic of the laser ablation process and the synthesis of the CQDs, depicting the charged nature of the quantum dots due to CO functionalization are shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), respectively.

The fragmentation was not adequate when the focused laser beam was maintained stationary at a single point inside the graphite dispersion. However, when the focal point was line-scanned in a repetitive fashion, significant fragmentation of the graphite particles was achieved leading to a stable water suspension of nearly uniform size carbon quantum dots. This is due to the fact that in the line scan, the probability of interaction of same graphitic microparticle with the focused laser beam is high within the interaction volume. This maximizes the heat dissipation rate resulting into significant fragmentation of graphite microcrystals into much smaller particles.

The CQDs suspensions were separated from treated graphite microparticle suspension by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 1 h. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, Japan and fei, Netherlands for high resolution TEM) was performed for size and morphology study on the samples prepared by drop casting the CQDs suspension onto a carbon coated copper grid and dried under an IR lamp for 20 min. Structural analysis was performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Ultima IV X-Ray Diffractometer) in the glancing angle configuration. For characterizing the material and phonon lines of resultant carbon nanostructures, Raman spectroscopy was performed on CQDs taken on a silicon substrate using a Renishaw in Via Raman microscope at 532 nm excitation in a backscattering geometry. Photoluminescence (PL) properties were determined by exciting the CQDs drop casted over a silicon substrate at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm from a HeCd laser. To investigate the presence of functional groups attached to the quantum dots, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using NICOLET iS50 FTIR. All measurements were carried out at ambient conditions and room temperature.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the optical images of CQDs water suspensions at the time of preparation (top) and after four months (bottom). A highly dispersed and stable suspension of CQDs with no agglomeration is evidently visible. The infrared absorption was unchanged for the samples even after four months period as seen from Fig. 2(b) where the FTIR spectra are shown for S80 sample taken at the time of preparation and after four months. No significant difference can be seen between the two spectra. Various functional groups were developed on the surface of CQDs during the laser ablation process. The absorption peaks at around 1068 cm−1 and 1140 cm−1 are assigned to the typical stretching modes of the COC and CO bonds, respectively. Absorption band at 1390 cm−1 corresponds to the CC stretching vibration and a broad peak feature at 1719 cm−1 is assigned to CO bond stretching [23–25]. The unsaturated -
C ¬ O
groups provide a net negative charge on the CQDs surface which helps in stability of the suspensions. Results from the Zeta potential measurements on S40 sample are presented in Fig. 2(c). It is found to be −13 mV. Thus the development of similar charges on CQDs surface leads to the repulsive columbic force between them that eventually prevents agglomeration of the quantum dots leading to a stable water suspension. The formation of the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs is crucial for achieving stable suspension either in water or in any other chemical solvent [26].

In the current study, the generation of CQDs of very small size (∼5 nm–15 nm) takes place due to laser induced fragmentation of graphite microcrystals. There are two possible mechanisms that take place during the laser induced fragmentation namely, melting-evaporation and Coulombic explosion [27–32]. The later process has been invoked frequently in the experiments employing ultrashort laser pulses such as picosecond and femtosecond pulses [32]. In the current work, the dominant mechanism in the formation of the CQDs was melting evaporation due to the high laser power provided by the ns laser at high repetition rate.

An intense laser beam with pulse fluence greater than the threshold for vaporization of suspended graphitic microcrystals generates highly excited plasma of electrons and ions as well as neutral atoms and molecules. Due to local heating within the photo excited microvolume of the sample, the highly energetic species of the plasma create a luminescent plume of extremely high temperature and pressure [33]. Thus generated plume expands adiabatically in ns time scale and simultaneously compresses the surrounding cold liquid medium locally resulting into generation of shockwaves at the plume/liquid interface. At the interface, the plume species loose energy due to initiation of thermalization in microsecond time-scale and deacceleration of particles begin. As a consequence, reverse pressure gradient develops at the interface resulting in an inward shockwave towards center of the plume. The process of inward and outward shock wave cycles from the interface continue until complete thermalization is reached leading to condensation, nucleation and clustering of the plume species in the gaseous phase, and formation of the CQDs.

The laser ablation of the graphitic microparticles in a surrounding liquid medium than air or vacuum is advantageous due to the ability to reach higher plasma temperature, pressure and density [33]. The water layer around the material plasma also gets heated up to similar temperatures implying formation of plasma of the liquid species within that layer. Mixing of the two plasmas allows chemical reactions among those species leading to natural functionalization of the quantum dots. Moreover, due to the repetitive scanning of the laser spot along a line of 1000 μmin the suspension, there is greater chance that the fragmented particles are laser ablated multiple times leading to smaller size particles. It is hence obvious why the same could not be achieved with stationary focused laser beam irradiation.

The morphology of the prepared CQDs was examined by TEM and XRD. From the TEM images as presented in Fig. 3 it can be seen that the average size and the size distribution of the CQDs become smaller with the increasing laser irradiation time. The average particle size was found to be ∼15 nm, ∼10 nm and ∼4.5 nm for S40, S60 and S80 samples, respectively. At much higher TEM resolution, the finer details within the quantum dots can be seen in Fig. 3 (d). The polycrystalline CQDs have crystalline regions of characteristic graphitic planes having inter-planar distance d of ∼0.38 nm. The selected area electron diffraction pattern is shown in Fig. 3 (e) that indicates the polycrystalline nature of the synthesized CQDs in our samples.

From the XRD data presented in Fig. 4 , the usual sharp and strong diffraction peak at angle ∼26.5° can be seen for highly crystalline graphitic microcrystals which is related to the (002) planes having an inter-planar spacing of d ∼ 0.35 nm. The related diffraction peak from CQDs is weaker and much broader (Fig. 4) and it is centered at an angle of ∼22.5°. The corresponding inter-planar spacing in the CQDs is estimated to be of ∼0.4 nm. From Fig. 4, we also notice that the width of the diffraction peak for the three CQDs samples increases with decreasing particle size. The broadening in the XRD spectra for the CQDs may be attributed to the polycrystalline nature of the CQDs and much importantly due to the presence of oxygenated groups which increase the spacing between the carbon planes [24,26].

Optical absorbance spectra of the CQDs suspensions as shown in Fig. 5 (a) evidently shows the characteristic broad absorption peaks at ∼230 and 270 nm. The lower wavelength peak is due to the π - π* transition of the core sp 2 carbon bonds, and the absorption at ∼270 nm corresponds to n - π* transition of the CO (or other oxygen functionalized groups) bond [25,34]. For S40 to S80, there is a blue shift in the peak position for both the 230 and 270 nm absorption bands indicating larger quantum confinement effects in smaller size particles. The CQDs were found to have strong green PL centered at around 535 nm when laser excited at 325 nm. The normalized PL spectra from all three CQDs samples are shown in Fig. 5(b). Owing to the presence of edge or surface states the PL emission spectra from the carbon nanostructures is usually expected to be broad [2,35]. This seems to be a good proposition for our observation as well. The broad PL spectrum of the CQDs is an integrated emission from aromatic core, surface states and various functional groups (CO, COC and CO) attached on the CQDs surface. Moreover, the structural deformation caused by either the functional groups or developed during laser ablation process can also lead to irregular mid energy states within the forbidden gap. These states might serve as intermediate relaxation pathways for the electrons during light emission on the red side of the broadened emission spectrum and therefore, causing an overall spectral heterogeneity in the emission spectrum [36–38].

Characteristic strong and sharp Raman line at frequency of ∼1580 cm−1 related to G-mode and a weak Raman line at frequency of ∼1350 cm−1 related to D-mode phonons are observed in the Raman spectrum of the laser untreated crystalline graphite microcrystals as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The Raman spectra for the CQD samples as shown in Fig. 6 (a) have a stronger and broader D-peak. Also, we notice from Fig. 6(a) that the characteristic G-peak related to in-plane vibration of the sp2 carbon atoms in graphite has developed a double peak nature in the CQDs. The Raman spectrum near the G-band for all the CQD samples can be fitted with two Lorentz oscillators centered at frequencies of ∼1530 cm−1 and ∼1598 cm−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(c).

The occurrence of such a double peak feature at the location of G-band in the CQDs has not been seen before, however, for graphene quantum dots, a similar but narrower double peak feature was reported in a few studies [39] and understanding of its origin is still open. Broader line-widths and a shift in the Raman peaks are usually due to size distribution of the CQDs. The broad G-band may also be indicative of the presence of an amorphous carbon shell around the polycrystalline quantum dots whereas, broader and more intense D-band in the CQDs can be attributed to the presence of an increased level of defects and disorder, and a decrease in the graphitic domain size [40,41]. From the Raman spectra in Fig. 6(a), we have estimated the ratio of the intensities of the D and G-modes [ID/IG] which is used as a measure of disorder in graphitic materials [5,6,42]. The ID/IG value for the initial crystalline graphite microparticles in powder is estimated to be ∼0.18 which increases to nearly 0.75 for the three CQD samples.

To summarize, ultrastable water dispersed fluorescent CQDs with controllable size were prepared by using nanosecond laser ablation of crystalline graphite microparticles. The stability of the CQDs water dispersions is due to the presence of similar charges developed on the surface/edges of the CQDs during the laser induced fragmentation. Quantum confinement is strongly evident from the optical absorption and strong green photoluminescence from the CQDs in which the absorption and the photoluminescence peaks blue-shift with decreasing the size of the quantum dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Department of science and technology, Government of India is acknowledged for financial support through project numbers YSS/2014/000880, IDP/MED/05/2014, ECR/2016/000022. AS acknowledges Council of Scientific and Industrial Research for Junior Research Fellowship.
